title: Abstract Classes & Interfaces
description: Decode the rules of abstract classes and interfaces — the OOP contracts that dominate CSE110 design questions and output-tracing exams.

sections.content: 📚 Why This Chapter Dominates CSE110 Exams

Abstract classes and interfaces are where Java OOP becomes truly powerful — and truly tricky to trace. Exams test whether you know: which methods **must** be implemented, what happens when a class inherits from an abstract class but misses an abstract method, how default methods in interfaces work, and how to resolve **diamond problem** conflicts. Every rule in this chapter has appeared in past CSE110 questions.
🏗️ Abstract Classes — Partial Blueprints

An **abstract class** is declared with the `abstract` keyword. It can contain **abstract methods** (signature only, no body) and **concrete methods** (with full implementation). You cannot instantiate an abstract class directly — you must subclass it and implement all abstract methods.

sections.title: Abstract Class with Abstract and Concrete Methods

sections.label: 🚨 Exam Trap: Partial Implementation of Abstract Class
sections.content: If a subclass of an abstract class does **not** implement ALL abstract methods, that subclass must also be declared `abstract`. Forgetting `abstract` on a partially implemented subclass is a **compile-time error**: *'class X is not abstract and does not override abstract method y() in Z'*. This exact error is tested in CSE110 find-error questions.
📋 Abstract Class Rules Reference

rows: Abstract class with NO abstract methods
Abstract class extending another abstract class
Compile error — abstract methods have no body
Concrete class with unimplemented abstract method
Compile error — static cannot be overridden

sections.content: 🔌 Interfaces — Pure Contracts

An **interface** is declared with the `interface` keyword. Traditionally, all its methods are implicitly `public abstract` and all its fields are implicitly `public static final`. A class **implements** an interface (not extends). Unlike inheritance, a class can implement **multiple interfaces** — this is Java's answer to multiple inheritance.

sections.title: Defining and Implementing an Interface

sections.content: ⚡ Default Methods in Interfaces (Java 8+)

Since Java 8, interfaces can have **default methods** — concrete methods with the `default` keyword. Implementing classes **inherit** the default method automatically without having to override it. This was introduced to allow adding new methods to interfaces without breaking existing implementations.

sections.title: Interface Default Method — Inherited and Overridable

sections.content: ⚠️ The Diamond Problem — Interface Conflict Resolution

When a class implements **two interfaces that both define a default method with the same signature**, a conflict arises. Java requires the implementing class to **explicitly override** the method to resolve the ambiguity. Inside the override, you can choose which interface's default to delegate to using `InterfaceName.super.method()`.

sections.title: Diamond Conflict — Forced Override Resolution

sections.label: Class Implementation Wins Over Interface Default
sections.content: If a class inherits a concrete method from a **superclass** AND implements an interface with a default method of the same signature, the **superclass method wins** automatically — no conflict, no override required. The priority order is: (1) concrete class method, (2) interface default method.
📊 Abstract Class vs Interface — Comparison Table
🔀 Combining Abstract Class and Interface

A common real-world pattern is an abstract class that **implements an interface partially**, leaving some methods for concrete subclasses. The abstract class satisfies some of the interface's contract, and concrete subclasses complete the rest.

sections.title: Abstract Class Partially Implementing an Interface

sections.content: 🔍 Interface as a Type — Polymorphism

An interface is a **reference type** — you can declare variables, parameters, and return types as interface types. Dynamic dispatch works just like with abstract classes: the actual runtime type determines which implementation runs.

sections.title: Interface Reference — Polymorphic Array of Shapes

sections.content: 🏋️ Interactive Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Abstract Method Dispatch Through Hierarchy
sections.explanation: Step 1: `obj.show()` — declared type Base, actual type Leaf. `show()` is concrete in Base, not overridden. Runs Base.show(). Inside Base.show(), `tag()` is called — dynamic dispatch → actual type Leaf → Leaf.tag() = 'LEAF'. Prints '[LEAF] Base.show'. Step 2: `((Mid) obj).display()` — downcast to Mid (valid: Leaf extends Mid). `display()` is concrete in Mid, not overridden by Leaf. Runs Mid.display(). Inside, `tag()` → dynamic dispatch → Leaf.tag() = 'LEAF'. Prints 'Mid.display: LEAF'.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Default Method — Inherited vs Overridden
sections.answer:
I say: Meow
I say: ...silent...
Dog says: Woof
sections.explanation: Cat: `introduce()` not overridden → uses Speak default. Inside, `voice()` → dynamic dispatch → Cat.voice() = 'Meow'. Prints 'I say: Meow'. Fish: `introduce()` not overridden → Speak default. `voice()` not overridden → Speak default = '...silent...'. Prints 'I say: ...silent...'. Dog: `introduce()` IS overridden → Dog.introduce() runs. Inside, `voice()` → Dog.voice() = 'Woof'. Prints 'Dog says: Woof'.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Concrete Class Missing Abstract Method
sections.error: WashingMachine class declaration: Compile-time error — WashingMachine is not abstract and does not override abstract method powerOff() in Appliance. Every abstract method from the parent must be implemented unless the subclass is itself declared abstract.
sections.explanation: A concrete class must implement ALL abstract methods inherited from its parent. WashingMachine only implements `powerOn()` but misses `powerOff()`. Fix option 1: add `powerOff()` implementation. Fix option 2: declare WashingMachine as abstract (defers `powerOff()` to WashingMachine's own subclasses). This is a **compile-time** error.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Interface Field Reassignment
sections.error: Line 9: Compile-time error — cannot assign a value to final variable MAX_RETRIES. All fields declared in an interface are implicitly `public static final`. Attempting to reassign them is a compile-time error, just like reassigning any `final` variable.
sections.explanation: Interface fields are always `public static final` regardless of whether you write those modifiers or not. `final` means the variable can only be assigned once — at declaration. Use interface constants as read-only values. If you need a mutable version, declare a regular instance or local variable initialized from the constant. This is a **compile-time** error.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Abstract Class Constructor Chain with Dynamic Dispatch
sections.answer:
Animal init, type=unknown
Lion init, type=Lion
Lion
sections.explanation: Step 1: `new Lion()` → Lion() calls implicit super() → Animal() constructor runs. In Animal(): `type='unknown'`. Then `getType()` is called — dynamic dispatch → actual type is Lion → Lion.getType() returns `type` which is currently 'unknown'. Prints 'Animal init, type=unknown'. Step 2: Animal() returns. Back in Lion(): `type='Lion'`. `getType()` → Lion.getType() = 'Lion'. Prints 'Lion init, type=Lion'. Step 3: main: `a.getType()` → Lion → 'Lion'. Prints 'Lion'. This illustrates the constructor + dynamic dispatch trap: abstract method called in parent constructor dispatches to the subclass override, but subclass fields are not yet initialized.

sections.title: Exercise 6: Design a Payment System with Abstract Class + Interface
sections.approach: Abstract class Payment provides the template method processPayment() which calls the abstract methods paymentMethod() and calculateTax(). Concrete subclasses fill in the details. The interface Taxable provides the TAX_RATE constant and the calculateTax() contract. CreditCard must implement both abstract methods from Payment and the abstract method from Taxable.
sections.explanation: This design shows the Template Method Pattern: `processPayment()` in the abstract class defines the algorithm structure, calling abstract methods `paymentMethod()` and `calculateTax()` whose implementations vary by subclass. CreditCard must implement: (1) `paymentMethod()` from abstract Payment, (2) `calculateTax()` from Taxable interface. Cash does the same with different logic. The polymorphic array allows processing all payment types with a single loop — open/closed principle: add new payment types without changing processPayment().

sections.content: 📋 Quick Reference Summary

items: **`abstract class`**: cannot be instantiated; can have constructors, any fields, concrete + abstract methods
**`abstract method`**: no body, no braces; subclass must override ALL of them or also be `abstract`
**`interface`**: all fields are `public static final`; all non-default methods are `public abstract`
**`implements`**: used to apply an interface to a class; a class can implement multiple interfaces
**`default` method**: concrete method in an interface; inherited automatically; can be overridden
**Diamond conflict**: two interfaces with same default method → implementing class MUST override it; use `Interface.super.method()` to delegate
**Priority**: concrete superclass method > interface default method (no override needed)
**Cannot**: create abstract static method; create abstract private method; instantiate abstract class or interface
**`@Override`**: use on all abstract method implementations; causes compile error if signature is wrong
**Dynamic dispatch rule**: applies equally to abstract class and interface method calls — actual runtime type determines which body runs
